Welcome back to The Varsity, my twice-weekly private email about
the power players who define the world of sports=E2=80=94and their delicate=
 egos.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

I spent the weekend in Bloomington, where I visited the famed Assembly Hall=
 for the first time. It=E2=80=99s not Cameron Stadium or Rupp Arena, but th=
e historic field house, the cauldron of those dynastic, pre-cancellation=E2=
=80=93Bobby Knight teams, is a special place to watch a game. The Universit=
y of Indiana
modernized the arena about a decade ago, and the changes don=E2=80=99t yet =
feel dated. Of course, it helped that my Terps pulled out a last-second win=
 to shock the Hoosiers=E2=80=A6

There won=E2=80=99t be any need to send me flowers on account of the Comman=
ders=E2=80=99 disappointing loss yesterday to the Eagles. The sting of defe=
at was somewhat salved by a long Wickersham and Van Natta joint in ESPN,
published ( https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/43538463/snyder-hates-comma=

The Starting Five: Super Bowl
Edition
-----------------------------

* Media scrum for next season: Super Bowl week has always doubled as a de f=
acto trade show for the sports business=E2=80=94a place where media and spo=
nsorship executives lobby the NFL honchos for, among other things, the best=
 games on next year=E2=80=99s schedule. This year, however, the league is h=
olding most of its network meetings before they get to New Orleans. (Networ=
k lobbying will continue, of course, right up to the moment the schedule is=
 released in
the spring.)

It=E2=80=99s easy to identify the games the networks most covet: anything i=
nvolving the Chiefs and (yes, still) the Cowboys. Next season=E2=80=99s blo=
ckbusters include an AFC Championship rematch (Chiefs at Bills) and a Super=
 Bowl rematch (Eagles at Chiefs). Another highly prized game will be Kansas=
 City at Dallas. Both the Chiefs and Cowboys also play the Lions=E2=80=94th=
ose are games that every network would want to carry.

Christmas Day, which falls on a Thursday this year, also poses an
interesting question. Amazon Prime is a few years into a decade-plus deal p=
aying about $1 billion per year to broadcast Thursday Night Football, while=
 Netflix has a three-year deal to carry games on Christmas for a few hundre=
d million bucks a pop. The NFL could schedule a triple-header, with Netflix=
 carrying two afternoon games and Amazon carrying one at night. But which s=
treamer would get the best game and window? We=E2=80=99ll see=E2=80=A6 Mean=
while, media executives will also be
probing the league for guidance on extending the regular season and the gro=
wth of the league=E2=80=99s international schedule, since Roger Goodell has=
 publicly stated he wants to expand both.
* Is there really Chiefs fatigue?: Don=E2=80=99t believe anything you read =
about the possibility of =E2=80=9CChiefs fatigue=E2=80=9D diminishing the S=
uper Bowl broadcast viewership. =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s a social media creati=
on,=E2=80=9D said one media executive, who laughed off any suggestion that
people might not watch the Super Bowl because they=E2=80=99re tired of seei=
ng Patrick Mahomes win all the time, or because they=E2=80=99re fed up with=
 the Taylor=E2=80=93Travis sideshow. Other sports business executives had s=
imilar responses.

Networks habitually crunch numbers to determine which NFL team is the most =
popular. This season, the Bills and the Lions entered that conversation, bu=
t neither got close to matching the Chiefs. =E2=80=9CTo me, the idea of
viewer fatigue is simply teams that turn from being seen as the good guy to=
 being seen as the bad guy,=E2=80=9D another executive told me. =E2=80=9CEv=
erybody seemed to love the Chiefs a couple of years ago. Now, there=E2=80=
=99s going to be a higher percentage of people hate-watching to root agains=
t them.=E2=80=9D

Two years ago, the Chiefs and Eagles set a Super Bowl record with more than=
 114 million viewers. Last year=E2=80=99s Chiefs-49ers overtime game broke =
that mark with more than 120 million. This year=E2=80=99s
numbers are hard to predict, but they=E2=80=99ll likely be even higher: Fox=
 will stream the game on Tubi, and the network brokered a deal with NBCUniv=
ersal to have Telemundo carry a Spanish-language feed. Meanwhile, Nielsen e=
xpanded its out-of-home coverage, which should allow the game to post big n=
umbers.
* Greg Olsen=E2=80=99s options: I=E2=80=99ve received emails from Varsity s=
ubscribers who are curious about Greg Olsen=E2=80=99s future in the
broadcast booth. Now that Tom Brady has said that he will return to the Fox=
 booth next season, what happens to the analyst who used to have that job a=
nd now holds up Fox=E2=80=99s number two booth?

The quick answer is that Olsen doesn=E2=80=99t have a lot of options if he =
wants to become a network=E2=80=99s number one analyst. Cris Collinsworth s=
igned an extension that keeps him on NBC=E2=80=99s Sunday Night Football un=
til the end of the decade. Troy Aikman,
Tony Romo, and Kirk Herbstreit still have multiple years left on their deal=
s. Unless the NFL expands and creates a new package, there=E2=80=99s nowher=
e for Olsen to go.
* Robot refs: Did Josh Allen extend forward enough to make a first down on =
that critical fourth-and-one play yesterday? CBS rules analyst Gene Sterato=
re sure thought so. The officials on the field disagreed, a
decision that spiked the Bills=E2=80=99 comeback hopes and played a signifi=
cant role in ending their season. That decision, itself, underscored the co=
mplaint that the NFL=E2=80=94a multi-multi-billion-dollar business=E2=80=94=
still relies on referees and a 10-yard chain link to determine the most imp=
ortant plays in the game. If tennis can use Hawk-Eye technology and basebal=
l has dopey robot umpires (sorry, =E2=80=9Cautomated strike zones=E2=80=9D)=
, why can=E2=80=99t the NFL put a microchip in the ball to be more precise =
about
where, exactly, to start each play?

Putting aside the technical question of whether it=E2=80=99s feasible (Joe =
Lemire says ( https://www.sportsbusinessjournal.com/Articles/2025/01/27/haw=
keye-nfl-first-down-chiefs-bills?publicationSource=3Dsbd&issue=3D9275fc0a2f=
6448d69797e291c380d6fa&utm_campaign=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F27%2=
F25%29&utm_content=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F27%2F25%29&utm_medium=
=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) no=
t yet) there=E2=80=99s been no momentum at the league level to make it happ=
en, according to my best sources. The biggest obstacle is that
any change must be approved by the notoriously slow competition committee, =
chaired by Falcons C.E.O. Rich McKay. Over the next two weeks, expect to he=
ar talking heads calling for the NFL to use the microchips. But you won=E2=
=80=99t see any movement on this from the league.
* Twins TV: Faced with the prospects of losing almost all of their local me=
dia rights fees, MLB teams like the Brewers and Reds signed deals with Main=
 Street
Sports (n=C3=A9e Diamond Sports Group) soon after the beleaguered R.S.N. pr=
ovider emerged from bankruptcy. But as MLB continues to try to develop a st=
rategy around its local rights, executives will certainly be paying close a=
ttention to Minnesota, where the Twins are forging ahead with their own pla=
n.

The Twins are offered a direct-to-consumer plan, which is priced more attra=
ctively than competitors in some other markets: $19.99 per month or $99 for=
 the season. (By comparison, NESN=E2=80=99s Red Sox
streams cost $29.99 per month and YES Network streams for $24.99.) The resu=
lts of this smaller-market experiment will be relevant to ESPN, which is ex=
pected to opt out of its current MLB deal at the end of this season and ren=
egotiate a new agreement that likely includes local rights to fill up its f=
orthcoming flagship streaming service.=C2=A0

If the Twins can demonstrate that there is a meaningful audience for local =
streaming, MLB will have the ammunition to bundle nearly all of its
teams=E2=80=94with the likely exception of the Yankees, Mets, Dodgers, Red =
Sox, and perhaps Braves, at least for now=E2=80=94and sell the rights to a =
major streamer at a premium. It=E2=80=99s still a big if, though=E2=80=A6

Facing the inexorable decline of their industry, the last of the cable cowb=
oys
are throwing their energy behind a not particularly new strategy: bundling.=
 In recent weeks, both DirecTV and Comcast have begun offering small packag=
es of news and live sports channels, priced below their basic subscription =
tiers=E2=80=94an offer eerily similar to the one that they wanted to destro=
y Venu for offering during its brief time on this planet of ours. Michael N=
athanson, a top analyst at his half-eponymous firm, MoffettNathanson, prais=
ed the
decisions, while arguing that the initial price points for those packages=
=E2=80=94they=E2=80=99re both around $70 a month=E2=80=94remain too high.

Nathanson joined me on the Varsity podcast over the weekend to discuss the =
harrowing path forward for both traditional distributors and the networks t=
hey carry. During our conversation, he told me that the industry should hee=
d the advice of John Malone, who=E2=80=99s advocated for distributors to ma=
intain a limited number of
live sports and news channels and put everything else on demand. (Alas, Mal=
one has a point, but markets rarely work that efficiently.) Below, I=E2=80=
=99ve excerpted a few of Nathanson=E2=80=99s choice insights from the full =
episode ( https://puck.news/podcast_episode/netflixs-ufc-gamble-espns-strea=
ming-calculus/?utm_campaign=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F27%2F25%29&u=

dited for clarity.

On the Cord-Cutting Trend
-------------------------

Nathanson: The research we=E2=80=99ve done has always
shown consumer interest for a sports-and-news-and-broadcast bundle of 40 to=
 50 channels for $40 to $50 per month. If you go back to the earliest days =
of those virtual M.V.P.D.s, the bundles were really cheap=E2=80=94and they =
were losing money. Their initial rollout had stopped the erosion of cord-cu=
tting because they were so attractively priced. But over the past three or =
four years, some of those products died, and the pricing started getting hi=
gher. So the consumer never had a choice of a $40-$50
product.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

I am somewhat happy to see both DirecTV and Comcast roll out these new prod=
ucts. But at $70, I think the price point is still too high. They still nee=
d to figure out some way to cut excess entertainment channels from that bun=
dle.

On Cable &
Satellite Pivots
----------------

Nathanson: There=E2=80=99s been more innovation in
the past year than we=E2=80=99ve seen during the past five years. People ar=
e starting to understand that you need to really change this bundle. We tho=
ught Venu was a good first step. I=E2=80=99m optimistic that people are try=
ing to figure this out, but the pessimistic side of me is saying the number=
 of bundled TV customers will not drop to zero. We always thought the floor=
 was 50 million, but that is tied in with the idea that people will work to=
gether to create a more efficient bundle.=C2=A0

Something that John Malone said to us, about two months ago, was that the w=
ay you should think about video going forward is that there is a live layer=
 that people value, but then there=E2=80=99s random-access entertainment co=
ntent that really should be on-demand. If you think about rebuilding these =
businesses with 20-30 live feeds, and then a bunch of truly on-demand conte=
nt, that=E2=80=99s streaming today. Consumers are still interested in payin=
g for video. But the delivery mechanism is
not what people want, and needs to change. Technology companies=E2=80=94lik=
e Amazon, Netflix, and YouTube=E2=80=94get it. It=E2=80=99s their natural D=
NA to get it and deliver consumer experience. The others have to really wor=
k hard on product and innovation.

Fox Sports=E2=80=99 Streaming
Future
-----------------------

Nathanson: We have been buyers of Fox stocks since the 2019
separation from Disney. So we=E2=80=99ve been really bullish on Fox because=
 of their strategy, which is: Don=E2=80=99t do anything stupid in streaming=
 and don=E2=80=99t blow your bundle. Last week, my colleague Robert Fishman=
 downgraded Fox to neutral for the first time, on the idea that cord-cuttin=
g could get worse. We thought the best idea was to create a skinny bundle w=
ith Fox, CBS Sports, Disney, TNT=E2=80=94the Venu approach, but with news a=
nd a little more sports. But now Venu
is dead and these sports bundles are emerging. It=E2=80=99s not clear what =
Fox does here.=C2=A0

Netflix=E2=80=99s Sports
Ambitions

Nathanson: I don=E2=80=99t see them going after the
AFC package. You know from the NBA negotiations that they were not really e=
ngaged in getting one of the three main packages. I don=E2=80=99t see them =

e, really=E2=80=94are in a weaker position relative to a partner. The leagu=
es need to be on Netflix because Netflix does bring that heat and that sizz=
le. The NFL can view Netflix as
a great way to watch the sport. But it may not be the moneymaker that Fox o=
r the CBS contract is.

Netflix doesn=E2=80=99t need sports to keep going. They need to keep innova=
ting and delivering great entertainment content with a little bit of sports=
=2E But they can make their own spor=
ts, as they did with the Tyson=E2=80=93Pa=
ul fight. I don=E2=80=99t consider them a true bidder for every single piec=

in

aying about $1 billion per year to broadcast <em>Thursday Night Football</e=

 see=E2=80=A6 Meanwhile, media executives will also be

"Margin:0 0 24px 0;"><em>Is there really Chiefs fatigue?</em>: Don=E2=80=99=

 but neither got <em>close</em> to matching the Chiefs. =E2=80=9CTo me, the=

<strong>Tony Romo</strong>, and <strong>Kirk Herbstreit</strong> still have=
 multiple years left on their deals. Unless the NFL expands and creates a n=

1" style=3D"Margin:0 0 24px 0;"><em>Robot refs</em>: Did <strong>Josh Allen=
</strong> extend forward enough to make a first down on that critical fourt=

 sure thought so. The officials on the field disagreed, a
decision that spiked the Bills=E2=80=99 comeback hopes and played a signifi=

 50 channels for $40 to $50 per month. If you go back to the earliest days =

tting because they were so attractively priced. But over the past three or =

gher. So the consumer never had a choice of a $40-$50
product.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin:0;">I am somewhat happy to see both DirecTV and Comcast roll o=

gin:0;"><br/>Something that John Malone said to us, about two months ago, w=

sted in paying for video. But the delivery mechanism is

NA to get it and deliver consumer experience. The others have to really wor=
